decisions.title: Use a centralized marketing content layer
decisions.decision: Keep reusable marketing copy, navigation, cards, and page content in content/site-content.ts.
decisions.reason: The site needs maintainable, CMS-friendly copy architecture without scattering text across many routes.
alternatives_considered: Hardcode copy inside each page
Introduce a full CMS before the MVP site was stable
decisions.impact: Makes copy and narrative changes faster and more consistent across pages.

decisions.title: Handle lead capture through a server-side Supabase route
decisions.decision: Public forms submit to /api/leads, which validates payloads and inserts into public.inbound_leads using the service-role key on the server.
decisions.reason: This keeps secrets server-side and preserves a clean, admin-friendly lead table.
alternatives_considered: Direct client-side Supabase inserts
Third-party form handlers
decisions.impact: Secure lead capture is live and can be expanded later for CRM workflows.

decisions.title: Use the apex domain as canonical host
decisions.decision: roofingrecyclers.com is the canonical public host, with www redirected to the apex domain.
decisions.reason: This keeps brand presentation and SEO canonicalization straightforward.
alternatives_considered: Use www as the primary canonical host
decisions.impact: Production routing, metadata, and middleware are aligned around the apex domain.

decisions.title: Frame public investor and policy copy carefully
decisions.decision: The site should present RAS, regulatory, manufacturer, and relationship advantages in strong but careful language without making unverified public claims.
decisions.reason: The site needs to feel credible and ambitious while avoiding overstatement.
alternatives_considered: Use more aggressive pitch-deck language directly on the public site
decisions.impact: Investor-facing copy is more durable for a public audience and easier to defend.

decisions.title: Adopt docs/project-memory as canonical durable memory location
decisions.decision: Initialize and maintain the project memory system inside docs/project-memory within the repo.
decisions.reason: This location is local, versioned, easy to discover, and does not require separate mirroring for this repo.
alternatives_considered: .project-memory
project-memory at repo root
decisions.impact: Future sessions can reconstruct project state directly from repo-backed JSON files.

decisions.title: Keep the home hero visual in a stacked layout
decisions.decision: The hero visual should avoid dense nested multi-column card layouts inside the constrained right-hand hero column.
decisions.reason: User feedback showed the previous nested layout made text look cramped and low-quality.
alternatives_considered: Keep nested side-by-side photo and card columns
Keep three mini-cards in a narrow row
decisions.impact: Home-page hero composition should remain roomier and more legible during future edits.

decisions.title: Version project memory immediately after initialization
decisions.decision: Commit and push docs/project-memory as soon as the initial memory system is created.
decisions.reason: The user's memory policy explicitly requires durable knowledge to be local-first and repo-backed, not left as untracked local files.
alternatives_considered: Leave memory files untracked until a later code change
Stage the files without committing
decisions.impact: The memory system is now reconstructible from the repo and remote backup, not just the local workspace.

decisions.title: Use a simplified stacked composition for the home hero visual
decisions.decision: The home hero visual should use a full-width photo block, short thesis cards, and stacked operating-sequence rows instead of narrow nested text boxes.
decisions.reason: Direct user screenshot feedback showed the prior hero composition still looked cramped and low-quality even after earlier spacing tweaks.
alternatives_considered: Keep the previous hero structure and only reduce font sizes
Keep mini-cards and photo-overlay copy inside the constrained right-hand column
decisions.impact: The hero now has clearer hierarchy and more room for industrial/investor-grade copy without overflow-style composition problems.

decisions.title: Send lead notifications as a best-effort step after Supabase insert
decisions.decision: Keep the existing Supabase insert as the primary success path and send SMTP email notifications only after the lead is stored, without failing the public submission if the mailer is unavailable.
decisions.reason: Lead capture is more important than notification delivery, and SMTP credentials may be absent or temporarily unavailable.
alternatives_considered: Fail the form submission if the notification email cannot be sent
Replace the DB insert with an email-only workflow
decisions.impact: Inbound leads remain safely stored in Supabase while the team can add email visibility without risking lost submissions.

decisions.title: Disable SMTP notifications again after failed Yahoo authentication
decisions.decision: When the supplied Yahoo credential failed SMTP authentication, remove SMTP_PASSWORD from local and Vercel production so the app returns to safe skip mode instead of repeatedly attempting bad logins.
decisions.reason: Repeated auth failures can trigger provider throttling or lockouts, and the site already preserves leads safely in Supabase without email delivery.
alternatives_considered: Leave the failing SMTP password active in production
Disable the entire lead submission path until email works
decisions.impact: Lead storage remains live while notifications stay paused until valid provider credentials are available.

decisions.title: Re-enable Yahoo SMTP only after direct credential verification
decisions.decision: A Yahoo app password should be verified directly against SMTP before it is restored in local or production config. Once verification succeeds, restore SMTP_PASSWORD, redeploy production, and keep the best-effort email design in place.
decisions.reason: Earlier bad credentials caused Yahoo auth failures, so production should only be re-enabled after a credential proves valid.
alternatives_considered: Write every new password directly into Vercel first and test afterward
Abandon Yahoo immediately instead of retrying with an app password
decisions.impact: SMTP is now active again with a verified Yahoo app password and a safer operational workflow for future rotations.

decisions.title: Label lead notifications as Roofing Recyclers Contact Form
decisions.decision: Use 'Roofing Recyclers Contact Form' as the visible sender name and subject prefix for lead-notification emails.
decisions.reason: The inbox notification should be immediately recognizable as a contact-form message rather than a generic system email.
alternatives_considered: Keep the generic '[Roofing Recyclers]' subject prefix
Change only the subject while leaving the sender as the raw Yahoo address
decisions.impact: Future contact-form emails should present more clearly in the inbox and be easier to search for by name.

decisions.title: Do not use external replyTo on Yahoo lead notifications
decisions.decision: When sending lead notifications through the current Yahoo SMTP mailbox, do not set the submitter email as a replyTo header. Keep the submitter email visible in the message body instead.
decisions.reason: A local SMTP matrix and a live production audit row both showed that Yahoo accepts simple and named-sender messages but rejects the same notification with an external replyTo header using '550 Request failed; Mailbox unavailable'.
alternatives_considered: Keep replyTo and assume inbox placement was the problem
Switch mail providers immediately before isolating the message-shape failure
decisions.impact: The mailer logic should prioritize deliverability over one-click reply behavior until a different provider or mailbox setup is chosen.

decisions.title: Use inbound_leads.notes as the notification audit trail
decisions.decision: Record notification delivery results in the existing inbound_leads.notes field rather than introducing a new table or schema change for the current MVP.
decisions.reason: The user needed immediate observability on sent vs failed notifications without schema drift or a longer migration cycle.
alternatives_considered: Add a dedicated notification_logs table
Rely only on server logs or inbox observation
decisions.impact: Production lead rows now preserve durable send/fail evidence, which makes future debugging faster and keeps the MVP schema stable.

decisions.title: Use light form controls on the dark marketing shell
decisions.decision: Shared form inputs, textareas, and selects should use a near-white background with dark text and readable option lists instead of dark translucent fields.
decisions.reason: Native dropdown surfaces were rendering with poor contrast against the previous dark-field treatment, making form options hard to read.
alternatives_considered: Keep dark fields and only special-case select text color
Patch the lead form component one field at a time
decisions.impact: All shared form controls now have reliable contrast on desktop and mobile, and future forms can reuse the same readable styling.
